title: Level 982
wikitext:

[[File:CODred.svg|250px|link=]]
The Trail Sign Says it All
{{Collapse|num=2|initial= {{User:Liminalityyyyy/Template:ClassicImageBox|file=Suprisinglystillup.jpg|text= |width=375px}} |final=
'''...'''
|body=
It hasn't been very long since you've set foot on the parks of another level. While it succumbs to still being part of the Backrooms and therefore not immune to all its wacky reality-bending anomalies, there is a clear juxtaposition between it and all the other places you've seen so far. There aren't too many places in the Backrooms where you can just sit down and take in all of the wonders that surround you. As long as you stay within the parks, you're in a more favorable position than the vast majority of this twisted reality. Yet these parks stretch out, and you can't help but scratch that itch that calls for adventure. Just admiring the urban beauty and the fresh air is not enough. You walk around for minutes, passing through wide avenues, apartment blocks, small forests, and even rivers. You see all these structures yet no vehicles that would help you travel across the level and see its many sights more conveniently. That is, until, you spot parked bikes in the distance. {{User:Liminalityyyyy/Template:ClassicImageBox|file=Itsnotpicturesque.jpg|text= |width=375px}}
The bikes were situated in an area which were within a forest, enclosed by the ever-reaching urban parks, an island of untouched nature in swathes of orderly artificiality. You notice the bikes are encrusted with rust, signs of wear and tear, and flimsy parts, but you assume that they are still in a comfortably serviceable state. Among all of the mildly decrepit bikes, one of them stands out to you. It is a bright yellow mountain bike, you think to yourself that these bikes will last longer than the others as it is built to survive the ruggedness and unpredictability of narrow, off-road trails. You take the bike, hop on it, then you go off to wherever your heart desires. You may stroll all throughout the parks and even beyond. From the extensive freeways to the blissful fields, the level is your oyster. Just avoid the concrete jungle, and don't go too hard on the olden bike.
'''However, they should be the least of your worries.'''
'''It's very naive of you to worry about them in the first place.'''
{{User:Liminalityyyyy/Template:ClassicImageBox|file=Trepxednoyeb.png|text= |width=375px}}
Beyond Expert. The trail sign says it all.


The sign taking on the shape of a crucifix is no accident. Whether that means that a merciful and loving God is here to protect you and is your only hope, or a foretelling of your untimely demise is anyone's guess. You wouldn't even tell there was a trail right ahead if you didn't look hard enough and had no context clues. You take one final look behind you, and notice the path blocked by forest. You can't just stay here and haplessly starve 'till the last calorie, and going off the barely beaten path is infeasible. You say one final prayer to the Lord, whether or not His love and protection extends to the Backrooms, and hope that you retain your fractured memories of mountain biking practice back home in the Frontrooms, and follow the trail on your bike. The first few stretches are unassuming. The trail switches back along a downward slope with some added jagged rocks, but it's not too-gut wrenching, especially since you're tapping back into your lost mountain biking expertise. The trail slowly unveils its true wrath onto you and your bike, as you encounter rocks that make the trail look like it's meant for hiking, not biking. Balance and momentum is key in these life and death situations, and you seem to fare well so far in these scenarios. Dealing with this terrain makes you lose faith in your bike, as its creaking loudens and its mechanisms grind. Some parts were definitely flimsier than when you first found the bike, like the handle and the seat, but those are only mild discomforts and your bike still serves you well. Five minutes into the trail, you see these thin boardwalks, which you would commonly see in biking endeavors back in the Frontrooms. They are no wider than a doormat and are supported by dead trees and branches, and thus their surface can be quite uneven. Sometimes they are just about as thin as a dinner plate. Their structure, their unpredictability, and your ability to master the two are questionable. Your mountain biking experience can only go so far. After some time, you would assume that you would get used to it, and that's where you're wrong. The trail surprises you every minute, having you strategize, control your fears, build your confidence and monitor your heart racing in split seconds. Slopes turn into drops. Jagged rocks chaining into boardwalks down a steep slope, having either of the two end in a jump, with every land leaving a scar on your bike's suspension. The moment your brain is focused on mastering momentum, the trail thins out to dead tree logs where balance is crucial. These logs are commonly chained with one another, testing your endurance, patience, and your ability at sharp and tight turns. Some of these logs end at a small jump or drop, the icing on the cake. The trail doesn't forget to mess with your mind, with it left thinking at times not knowing what constitutes as the trail and what isn't. You veered off the path without realizing, but fortunately you correct your mistake before you go out of bounds. The thought of possibly ending up in places like [[Level 777|this]] for these mishaps crosses your mind, but you're still going strong. Fallen trees begin hindering the trail, rendering bridges unpassable. At least there's a short detour, but there's a sensation that you can't put your finger on for taking them. Some of the trees force you to duck as much as you can while riding the bike, where you are mere centimeters away from what is at least a serious injury. You wouldn't want that in a setting like this... But then, for ten minutes straight, nothing happens. You still see boardwalks and logs, jagged terrain, and debris, but there's no change. It's all the same stuff for ten minutes. Your body and mind is adjusting to all of this rigor, your only real worry is the durability of the bike. Perhaps in a few minutes, your bike will finally give way after a hard landing? Then as you try to continue on foot, what repercussion is there to be had when you try to continue on foot? Fortunately, your bike doesn't shatter into several fragments, however that latter question about being on foot which had crossed your mind would then be answered. In the distance, you hear the sounds of mountain air. The winds that you would hear when you're at the top of the world. Why would you hear something like this all down here? Then, it hits you. You see a gaping foggy chasm about as wide as a car is long, with no end in sight. There are ramps on either side of the chasm. Just after the ramp ends on the other side, there's a sharp turn. You have to balance your speed and momentum. Having too little speed will bring a fate which needs no introduction, and having too much and you'll crash into the forest, messing up both your body and bike in hundreds of ways. You look behind, only to see the exact same sight instead of the trail you've been riding along for the past thirty minutes. All you have is a wing and a prayer. After calming down your heartbeat and mustering the courage, you finally take the leap of faith. Miraculously, you had made it to the other side in one piece. It's a one and done ordeal, it's over now. Your heart was still beating very fast, though it is starting to slowly calm down. Just a few rocks and boardwalks to go through now. Then, after all that, the trail smoothens and straightens out. Even the forest's density eases out. Then, after some time, you arrive at a dark, foreboding tunnel. [[Level 193|It]] awaits on the other side.
